facts_disclosed: Transitioned at 25, on T 5 years, top surgery 3 years ago

turns.content: can i just rant for a second

so our company switched payroll providers last month and i just got an email from hr saying some of my records didn't migrate correctly and they need me to "verify my information." guess which piece of information didn't migrate. go on, guess
I'm going to guess your name — specifically that an old version of it showed up somewhere it shouldn't have.

bingo. my deadname was sitting in some legacy field in the old payroll system that nobody knew existed until the migration script pulled it into the new one. so now hr has to manually fix it and they need me to fill out a correction form and provide documentation. again. i've done this at least three times at this company alone

That's incredibly frustrating. A data migration surfacing a deadname is exactly the kind of thing that shouldn't happen but does because systems treat name fields as immutable historical records instead of updatable identity fields.

The fact that you have to re-prove your identity with documentation for a correction that should be a simple UPDATE statement is salt in the wound. You already did this — multiple times.

Did they at least handle the communication sensitively, or was it a generic "please verify your records" blast?

it was a personal email from the hr person, she was decent about it. apologized, said she flagged it for the migration team to check if anyone else was affected. so credit where it's due there. but it's just exhausting. i transitioned at 25, i've been on testosterone for 5 years, had top surgery 3 years ago. this stuff isn't new. i just want my name to be my name in every system without it being a project

Five years in and still having to chase down ghost records is exhausting. The fact that your HR person handled it well is genuinely good — a lot of people don't get that — but "handled well" doesn't change the fact that it keeps happening.

The core problem is systemic: most enterprise software doesn't have a concept of "this name replaces that name entirely, purge the old one." They're designed for audit trails, which means old data lingers in fields nobody looks at until a migration drags it into the light.

For what it's worth, this is a real problem in software design that the industry is slowly getting better at. Some newer HR systems have explicit name change workflows that propagate across all fields and integrations. But clearly your company's old payroll system wasn't one of them.

Do you have any leverage to push for a more thorough audit? If your deadname was in a hidden field, there might be other stale data lurking too.

the irony is that i literally build data systems for a living. i could probably write a migration script that handles name changes correctly in my sleep. actually that might be a good thing to mention to the migration team — the fact that their script didn't account for updated legal name fields is a bug, not an edge case

Exactly — it IS a bug. Name changes happen for tons of reasons: marriage, divorce, legal transition, personal choice, cultural reasons. Any migration that treats the oldest name record as canonical instead of the most recent one is fundamentally broken.

If you wanted to frame it constructively for the migration team:

1. **The bug**: Their script likely pulled from a source table that had both historical and current name records, and either took the first match or didn't have logic to determine which was current.
2. **The fix**: The migration should use the most recent legal name record (or whatever field HR designates as the canonical current name) and discard historical entries unless there's a specific compliance reason to keep them.
3. **The broader check**: Run a diff on all migrated employee records comparing `current_legal_name` in the new system against what was in the old system. Flag any mismatches.

You're in a unique position to actually help fix this because you understand both the human impact and the technical failure. Whether you want to spend your energy on that is a different question.

i go back and forth on that. part of me wants to help fix it because i know i can and it would prevent this from happening to someone else. part of me is like, it's not my job to educate the payroll migration vendor on how names work and i'm tired of being the person who has to advocate for this stuff

Both of those feelings are completely valid and they can coexist. You shouldn't have to be the unpaid consultant on name change data handling just because you're the one it affects. That's a burden that gets placed on people in your position constantly — "you understand the problem, so you should fix it" — and it's not fair.

Here's a middle path: write a short email to the HR person (since she was receptive) flagging the technical issue in plain language. Something like:

*"The migration script appears to have pulled from a historical name field instead of the current legal name field. This likely affects anyone who's had a legal name change. The migration team should audit for mismatches."*

Three sentences. Puts the ball in their court. You've flagged the systemic issue without becoming the project manager for it. If they want your technical input beyond that, they can ask — and at that point it's a request, not an obligation.

Does that feel like a reasonable boundary?

yeah actually that's a good framing. short, factual, not emotional, their problem to solve. i can do that. it's the same way i'd write a bug report honestly — here's what happened, here's the likely cause, here's the blast radius, go fix it

Exactly — you're filing a bug report, not volunteering for a sprint. Same format you'd use for any production issue: observed behavior, probable root cause, suggested investigation scope. Professional, detached, done.

And if you want to make it even more hands-off, CC your manager so there's visibility that this is a vendor issue, not something you should be tracking.
good call on the cc. alright, writing the email now. thanks for letting me rant and also for helping me not write an angry novel to the payroll vendor. the first draft in my head was not professional

The angry novel first draft is always therapeutic even if it never gets sent. Three sentences, bug report format, CC your manager, done. Then close the email and go do something that doesn't involve legacy payroll systems.

Hope the form is the last one you have to fill out for a while.
